text:
{
  "instructions":"Write your name and index number on the answer booklet as written on your registration form. Do not open this paper until you are told to do so. This paper consists of three sections A, B and C. Answer ALL questions in section A. Answer THREE questions in section B. Answer only one question in section C. Use only a blue or black pen.",
   "answerKey":{
      "1":"A, C and D because they don't have a cell wall, a large central vacuole and chloroplasts.",
      "2":"Part | Plant cell | Animal cell\nNucleus | √ | √\nCell membrane | √ | √\nCytoplasm | √ | √\nCell wall | √ | X\nLarge vacuole | √ | X\nChloroplasts | √ | X",
      "3":"a) Plasma: transports nutrients, urea, hormones, blood cells...\nb) Red blood cells: transports oxygen and carbon dioxide.\nc) White blood cells: body defence, production of antibodies.\nd) Platelets: blood clotting.",
      "4":"a) Saprophytes are organisms which feed on dead decaying organic matter while parasites depend on other organisms for both food and shelter. A parasite is an organism which benefits from another (host) causing harm to the host.\nb) i. Malaria is caused by Plasmodium.\nii. Sleep under mosquito nets; remove stagnant water around homes; spray with insecticides; remove bushes around houses.",
      "5":"a) Xylem: water and mineral salts\nPhloem: water, sucrose, amino acids\nb) Water, blood proteins, glucose, fats, amino acids, salts, vitamins, carbon dioxide, blood cells, oxygen, hormones, waste products.",
      "6":"a) Because cells are partly made of proteins and would be broken down into digestion and would cause ulcers.\nb) This is a wrong PH for the enzymes in the gut because they would be denatured by acidity and would not work or the enzymes of the small intestines work at basic (alkaline) PH.",
      "7":"a) Lipase breaks down lipids facilitated by bile salts of bile to fatty acids and glycerol. The emulsification of fats increases the surface area of lipids; this facilitates the activity of lipase enzymes. Fatty acids are acidic and lowers the PH.\nb) Because there was no bile to emulsify the lipids for easy digestion or because there is no bile to facilitate lipase action.",
      "8":"Excretion of water is a metabolic product and is removed when in excess and osmoregulation in water is maintained to keep the water potential of blood more or less constant or to maintain water balance, maintain normal blood concentration.",
      "9":"Its body temperature is the same as or only a few degrees above its surroundings. This makes it very dependent on temperature changes. Or the temperature of the fish changes depending on the surroundings.",
      "10":"a) A zygote is a product of fusion of male and female reproductive cells. All other cells of the body arises from the mitotic division of the zygote.\nb) A virus when outside a cell cannot grow or reproduce. It does not feed, respire or excrete. These are characteristics of living things. It is for this reason that it is difficult to say whether it is living or not. The only living characteristic it shows is the ability to reproduce when inside a living cell, using materials supplied by the cells.",
      "11":"a) P - Epithelium (blood capillary network); Q - Lacteal (lymphatic vessel)\nb) P absorbs amino acids and glucose and glucose/vitamins/mineral salts. Or transport, glucose/amino acids /mineral salts. Q absorbs fatty acids and glycerol.\nc) The lining of the epithelium is very thin or permeable membrane, extensive capillary network, rich blood supply etc.",
      "12":"A - incisors, B - Canine, C - Carnassials or molars",
      "13":"i) nucleus, ii) chromosomes, iii) genes, iv) tissues, v) organs, vi) organisms",
      "14":"a) Maintenance of blood flow in a mammal\n• The automatic stimulation of the pace maker that enables the continuous contraction of the cardiac muscles that allows the pumping of blood out of the heart to the rest of the body parts.\n• Difference in ventricular muscles which enables blood to be pumped out of the heart at different pressures depending on the distance to be covered.\n• Elasticity of the arteries that allows constrictions which maintains high pressure in arteries.\n• Skeletal contractions maintain movement of blood in the veins.\n• Thicker walls of arteries which maintain and withstand high pressure of blood.\n• Presence of valves in veins and the heart that prevent the back flow of blood.\n• Continuous blood supply to the heart muscles by coronary arteries which enables respiration for energy supply to allow the heart contractions.\nb) • Transpiration pull\n• Root pressure\n• Capillary force\n• Osmotic pressure\n• Cohesion force\n• Adhesion force.",
      "15":"a) i. Hormones are organic substances secreted in minute quantities into the blood stream by endocrine glands or specialized nerve cells and regulate the growth or functioning of specific tissue.\nii. Endocrine glands\nb) Insulin, glucagon\nc) When the blood glucose level increases, the beta cells of the pancreas releases insulin hormone. Insulin facilitates conversion of glucose to glycogen and fats, oxidation to glucose. The blood glucose level decreases to the set point. When the blood glucose level decreases, the alpha cells of the pancreas releases glucagon hormone. Glucagon stimulates the conversion of glycogen to glucose, fats to glucose, amino acids to glucose. The blood glucose level increases to the set point.",
      "16":"a) Sexual contact with an infected person, use of hypodermic needles contaminated with infected blood, transfusion of infected blood, homosexual partners with infected people, drug users share needles and syringes, infected mothers can infect the foetus.\nb) Use of condoms during sexual intercourse, setting up free needle schemes for those people who inject drugs, screening blood before transfusion, education programs to make people aware of the methods of transmission of the HIV virus and how they can be prevented, abstaining from sexual intercourse, faithfulness, avoid drug use, avoid blood contact during accidents, proper delivery of babies during birth (PMTCT).",
      "17":"Binary fusion, spore formation, vegetative propagation, natural layering, rhizomes, tubers, cutting, fragmentation, budding, grafting, suckers, cloning etc.",
      "18":"a) Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the stamen to the stigma of a flower.\nb) Self-pollination, cross pollination.\nc) Characteristics of self-pollination: • Hermaphrodite flowers\n• Anthers and carpels get mature simultaneously,\n• Anthers are above the stigma\n• Petals remain closed until pollination takes place\n• Self-compatibility.\nCharacteristics of cross pollination: • Anthers mature before stigma\n• Self-sterility\n• Stigma matures before anthers",
      "19":"a) i. Osmosis\nii. It is the movement of water molecules from their highest concentration to the lowest concentration to the lowest concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.\nb) i. The water level will rise in the capillary tube.\nii. The solution inside the dialysis tubing exerts osmotic pressure on the water in the beaker and water molecules move from the beaker where there are more water molecules through the dialysis tubing which is acting as a semi-permeable membrane. This will continue until the salts solution is diluted enough. The water level in the beaker will also fall.\nc) Water enters both cells because there are more water molecules in the beaker than there are in the cells. The animal cell will eventually swell until it bursts because its cell membrane cannot resist the internal pressure inside the cell. The plant cell however will not burst because it has a cell wall outside the cell membrane which is strong enough to resist the internal pressure inside the cell."
   },
   "allowSelectiveAnswering":true,
   "sectionBRequiredQuestions":3,
   "sectionCRequiredQuestions":1,
   "sections":[
      {
         "name":"A",
         "description":"Multiple Choice, True/False, and Fill-in-the-Blank Questions",
         "questions":[
            {
               "questionNumber":1,
               "text":"The diagram below represents types of cells.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"[Diagram A: An animal cell showing a nucleus and cell membrane.]\n[Diagram B: A plant cell showing a cell wall, cell membrane, and nucleus.]\n[Diagram C: A bacterial cell showing a cell membrane.]\n[Diagram D: A sperm cell showing a cell membrane and nucleus.]",
               "correctAnswer":"A, C and D because they don't have a cell wall, a large central vacuole and chloroplasts.",
               "points":3
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":2,
               "text":"Using √ and X, indicate the parts of the cell found in a plant and animal cell.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"Part | Plant cell | Animal cell\nNucleus | √ | √\nCell membrane | | \nCytoplasm | | \nCell wall | | \nLarge vacuole | | \nChloroplasts | | ",
               "correctAnswer":"Part | Plant cell | Animal cell\nNucleus | √ | √\nCell membrane | √ | √\nCytoplasm | √ | √\nCell wall | √ | X\nLarge vacuole | √ | X\nChloroplasts | √ | X",
               "points":5
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":3,
               "text":"Blood contains plasma, red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets. Give the function of each:",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Plasma",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"transports nutrients, urea, hormones, blood cells...",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Red blood cells",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"transports oxygen and carbon dioxide.",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"White blood cells",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"body defence, production of antibodies.",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"d)",
                     "text":"Platelets",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"blood clotting.",
                     "points":1
                  }
               ],
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":4,
               "text":"a) Distinguish between saprophytes and parasites.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Distinguish between saprophytes and parasites.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Saprophytes are organisms which feed on dead decaying organic matter while parasites depend on other organisms for both food and shelter. A parasite is an organism which benefits from another (host) causing harm to the host.",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"i) Name a common disease in Rwanda caused by protozoa.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Malaria is caused by Plasmodium.",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b) ii)",
                     "text":"Suggest methods that can be used to control the disease you named in b (i).",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Sleep under mosquito nets; remove stagnant water around homes; spray with insecticides; remove bushes around houses.",
                     "points":3
                  }
               ],
               "points":6
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":5,
               "text":"What substances are transported by:",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"The vascular systems of a flowering plant?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Xylem: water and mineral salts\nPhloem: water, sucrose, amino acids",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"The blood system of mammals?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Water, blood proteins, glucose, fats, amino acids, salts, vitamins, carbon dioxide, blood cells, oxygen, hormones, waste products.",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":6,
               "text":"a) Why is mucus needed to protect the cell lining the gut from protein-digesting enzymes?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Why is mucus needed to protect the cell lining the gut from protein-digesting enzymes?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Because cells are partly made of proteins and would be broken down into digestion and would cause ulcers.",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"The pH in the stomach is very acidic about pH 2. Why would it be a problem if the pH in the small intestines was 2?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"This is a wrong PH for the enzymes in the gut because they would be denatured by acidity and would not work or the enzymes of the small intestines work at basic (alkaline) PH.",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":7,
               "text":"An experiment was done to investigate effects of bile on lipid digestion.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"Lipase enzyme and bile were added to a lipid test tube A. Test tube B only had the lipid and lipase enzyme.",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Explain why the pH in test tube A became acidic.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Lipase breaks down lipids facilitated by bile salts of bile to fatty acids and glycerol. The emulsification of fats increases the surface area of lipids; this facilitates the activity of lipase enzymes. Fatty acids are acidic and lowers the PH.",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Explain why the reaction in test tube B was very slow.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Because there was no bile to emulsify the lipids for easy digestion or because there is no bile to facilitate lipase action.",
                     "points":1
                  }
               ],
               "points":3
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":8,
               "text":"Explain why the elimination of water by the kidney may be considered to be both excretion and osmoregulation.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "correctAnswer":"Excretion of water is a metabolic product and is removed when in excess and osmoregulation in water is maintained to keep the water potential of blood more or less constant or to maintain water balance, maintain normal blood concentration.",
               "points":4
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":9,
               "text":"Why is it more accurate to describe fish as a ‘variable-temperature’ animal rather than cold-blooded?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "correctAnswer":"Its body temperature is the same as or only a few degrees above its surroundings. This makes it very dependent on temperature changes. Or the temperature of the fish changes depending on the surroundings.",
               "points":2
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":10,
               "text":"a) How is a zygote different from any other cell in the body?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"How is a zygote different from any other cell in the body?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A zygote is a product of fusion of male and female reproductive cells. All other cells of the body arises from the mitotic division of the zygote.",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Why is it difficult to decide whether viruses are living organisms?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"A virus when outside a cell cannot grow or reproduce. It does not feed, respire or excrete. These are characteristics of living things. It is for this reason that it is difficult to say whether it is living or not. The only living characteristic it shows is the ability to reproduce when inside a living cell, using materials supplied by the cells.",
                     "points":3
                  }
               ],
               "points":5
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":11,
               "text":"The diagram below shows a villus in longitudinal section from ileum of a mammal.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"[Diagram of a villus with a scale bar labeled 0.2 mm. Label P points to the capillary network. Label Q points to the lacteal.]",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Name the parts labelled P and Q.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"P - Epithelium (blood capillary network); Q - Lacteal (lymphatic vessel)",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"What are the functions of P and Q?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"P absorbs amino acids and glucose and glucose/vitamins/mineral salts. Or transport, glucose/amino acids /mineral salts. Q absorbs fatty acids and glycerol.",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"Explain how P is adapted to carry out its functions.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"The lining of the epithelium is very thin or permeable membrane, extensive capillary network, rich blood supply etc.",
                     "points":2
                  }
               ],
               "points":6
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":12,
               "text":"The diagram below shows the side view of a skull of a carnivore.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"[Diagram of a carnivore skull in profile. Label A points to the canine tooth. Label B points to the carnassial tooth. Label C points to the incisor tooth.]",
               "correctAnswer":"A - incisors, B - Canine, C - Carnassials or molars",
               "points":2
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":13,
               "text":"Choose words from the list to complete the sentences that follow.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"Cells; Chloroplast; Chromosomes; Genes; Organs; Organisms; Nucleus; Tissues.",
               "correctAnswer":"i) nucleus, ii) chromosomes, iii) genes, iv) tissues, v) organs, vi) organisms",
               "points":6
            }
         ]
      },
      {
         "name":"B",
         "description":"Attempt any THREE questions.",
         "questions":[
            {
               "questionNumber":14,
               "text":"Briefly explain how:",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"The flow of blood is maintained in a mammal.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Maintenance of blood flow in a mammal\n• The automatic stimulation of the pace maker that enables the continuous contraction of the cardiac muscles that allows the pumping of blood out of the heart to the rest of the body parts.\n• Difference in ventricular muscles which enables blood to be pumped out of the heart at different pressures depending on the distance to be covered.\n• Elasticity of the arteries that allows constrictions which maintains high pressure in arteries.\n• Skeletal contractions maintain movement of blood in the veins.\n• Thicker walls of arteries which maintain and withstand high pressure of blood.\n• Presence of valves in veins and the heart that prevent the back flow of blood.\n• Continuous blood supply to the heart muscles by coronary arteries which enables respiration for energy supply to allow the heart contractions.",
                     "points":5
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"The flow of water is maintained in a flowering plant.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"• Transpiration pull\n• Root pressure\n• Capillary force\n• Osmotic pressure\n• Cohesion force\n• Adhesion force.",
                     "points":5
                  }
               ],
               "points":10
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":15,
               "text":"a) i) What are hormones?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a) i)",
                     "text":"What are hormones?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Hormones are organic substances secreted in minute quantities into the blood stream by endocrine glands or specialized nerve cells and regulate the growth or functioning of specific tissue.",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"a) ii)",
                     "text":"Where are hormones produced?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Endocrine glands",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Which hormones are produced by the pancreas?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Insulin, glucagon",
                     "points":5
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"Explain how the blood sugar level in blood is controlled.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"When the blood glucose level increases, the beta cells of the pancreas releases insulin hormone. Insulin facilitates conversion of glucose to glycogen and fats, oxidation to glucose. The blood glucose level decreases to the set point. When the blood glucose level decreases, the alpha cells of the pancreas releases glucagon hormone. Glucagon stimulates the conversion of glycogen to glucose, fats to glucose, amino acids to glucose. The blood glucose level increases to the set point.",
                     "points":5
                  }
               ],
               "points":12
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":16,
               "text":"a) How is AIDS transmitted from one person to another?",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"How is AIDS transmitted from one person to another?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Sexual contact with an infected person, use of hypodermic needles contaminated with infected blood, transfusion of infected blood, homosexual partners with infected people, drug users share needles and syringes, infected mothers can infect the foetus.",
                     "points":4
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Suggest all possible methods that can be used to avoid the AIDS virus.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Use of condoms during sexual intercourse, setting up free needle schemes for those people who inject drugs, screening blood before transfusion, education programs to make people aware of the methods of transmission of the HIV virus and how they can be prevented, abstaining from sexual intercourse, faithfulness, avoid drug use, avoid blood contact during accidents, proper delivery of babies during birth (PMTCT).",
                     "points":6
                  }
               ],
               "points":10
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":17,
               "text":"Identify the various modes of asexual reproduction in plants and animals.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "correctAnswer":"Binary fusion, spore formation, vegetative propagation, natural layering, rhizomes, tubers, cutting, fragmentation, budding, grafting, suckers, cloning etc.",
               "points":10
            },
            {
               "questionNumber":18,
               "text":"a) Define pollination.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a)",
                     "text":"Define pollination.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the stamen to the stigma of a flower.",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b)",
                     "text":"Name the types of pollination and characteristics of each.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Self-pollination, cross pollination.\nCharacteristics of self-pollination: • Hermaphrodite flowers\n• Anthers and carpels get mature simultaneously,\n• Anthers are above the stigma\n• Petals remain closed until pollination takes place\n• Self-compatibility.\nCharacteristics of cross pollination: • Anthers mature before stigma\n• Self-sterility\n• Stigma matures before anthers",
                     "points":9
                  }
               ],
               "points":10
            }
         ]
      },
      {
         "name":"C",
         "description":"Compulsory question.",
         "questions":[
            {
               "questionNumber":19,
               "text":"Some students set the experiment below to investigate a biological process.",
               "type":"open-ended",
               "passage":"[Diagram: A beaker containing water. Inside the beaker is a thistle funnel with a capillary tube inserted into its stem. The bottom of the funnel is covered by a membrane labeled 'Dialysis tubing containing concentrated salt solution'. An arrow points to the liquid level in the capillary tube labeled 'Level of solution at the start of the investigation'. Labels include 'Capillary tube', 'Beaker', and 'Water'.]",
               "subQuestions":[
                  {
                     "label":"a) i)",
                     "text":"Name the process being investigated.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Osmosis",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"a) ii)",
                     "text":"Define the process named in a(i) above.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"It is the movement of water molecules from their highest concentration to the lowest concentration to the lowest concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.",
                     "points":2
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b) i)",
                     "text":"What will happen to the water level in the capillary tube during this investigation?",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"The water level will rise in the capillary tube.",
                     "points":1
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"b) ii)",
                     "text":"Explain what has happened in b(i) above.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"The solution inside the dialysis tubing exerts osmotic pressure on the water in the beaker and water molecules move from the beaker where there are more water molecules through the dialysis tubing which is acting as a semi-permeable membrane. This will continue until the salts solution is diluted enough. The water level in the beaker will also fall.",
                     "points":5
                  },
                  {
                     "label":"c)",
                     "text":"Explain fully what happens when both animal and plant cells are put in beaker containing pure water.",
                     "type":"open-ended",
                     "correctAnswer":"Water enters both cells because there are more water molecules in the beaker than there are in the cells. The animal cell will eventually swell until it bursts because its cell membrane cannot resist the internal pressure inside the cell. The plant cell however will not burst because it has a cell wall outside the cell membrane which is strong enough to resist the internal pressure inside the cell.",
                     "points":6
                  }
               ],
               "points":15
            }
         ]
      }
   ]
}

parsedContent.instructions: Write your name and index number on the answer booklet as written on your registration form. Do not open this paper until you are told to do so. This paper consists of three sections A, B and C. Answer ALL questions in section A. Answer THREE questions in section B. Answer only one question in section C. Use only a blue or black pen.

answerKey.1: A, C and D because they don't have a cell wall, a large central vacuole and chloroplasts.
answerKey.2:
Part | Plant cell | Animal cell
Nucleus | √ | √
Cell membrane | √ | √
Cytoplasm | √ | √
Cell wall | √ | X
Large vacuole | √ | X
Chloroplasts | √ | X
answerKey.3:
a) Plasma: transports nutrients, urea, hormones, blood cells...
b) Red blood cells: transports oxygen and carbon dioxide.
c) White blood cells: body defence, production of antibodies.
d) Platelets: blood clotting.
answerKey.4:
a) Saprophytes are organisms which feed on dead decaying organic matter while parasites depend on other organisms for both food and shelter. A parasite is an organism which benefits from another (host) causing harm to the host.
b) i. Malaria is caused by Plasmodium.
ii. Sleep under mosquito nets; remove stagnant water around homes; spray with insecticides; remove bushes around houses.
answerKey.5:
a) Xylem: water and mineral salts
Phloem: water, sucrose, amino acids
b) Water, blood proteins, glucose, fats, amino acids, salts, vitamins, carbon dioxide, blood cells, oxygen, hormones, waste products.
answerKey.6:
a) Because cells are partly made of proteins and would be broken down into digestion and would cause ulcers.
b) This is a wrong PH for the enzymes in the gut because they would be denatured by acidity and would not work or the enzymes of the small intestines work at basic (alkaline) PH.
answerKey.7:
a) Lipase breaks down lipids facilitated by bile salts of bile to fatty acids and glycerol. The emulsification of fats increases the surface area of lipids; this facilitates the activity of lipase enzymes. Fatty acids are acidic and lowers the PH.
b) Because there was no bile to emulsify the lipids for easy digestion or because there is no bile to facilitate lipase action.
answerKey.8: Excretion of water is a metabolic product and is removed when in excess and osmoregulation in water is maintained to keep the water potential of blood more or less constant or to maintain water balance, maintain normal blood concentration.
answerKey.9: Its body temperature is the same as or only a few degrees above its surroundings. This makes it very dependent on temperature changes. Or the temperature of the fish changes depending on the surroundings.
answerKey.10:
a) A zygote is a product of fusion of male and female reproductive cells. All other cells of the body arises from the mitotic division of the zygote.
b) A virus when outside a cell cannot grow or reproduce. It does not feed, respire or excrete. These are characteristics of living things. It is for this reason that it is difficult to say whether it is living or not. The only living characteristic it shows is the ability to reproduce when inside a living cell, using materials supplied by the cells.
answerKey.11:
a) P - Epithelium (blood capillary network); Q - Lacteal (lymphatic vessel)
b) P absorbs amino acids and glucose and glucose/vitamins/mineral salts. Or transport, glucose/amino acids /mineral salts. Q absorbs fatty acids and glycerol.
c) The lining of the epithelium is very thin or permeable membrane, extensive capillary network, rich blood supply etc.
answerKey.12: A - incisors, B - Canine, C - Carnassials or molars
answerKey.13: i) nucleus, ii) chromosomes, iii) genes, iv) tissues, v) organs, vi) organisms
answerKey.14:
a) Maintenance of blood flow in a mammal
• The automatic stimulation of the pace maker that enables the continuous contraction of the cardiac muscles that allows the pumping of blood out of the heart to the rest of the body parts.
• Difference in ventricular muscles which enables blood to be pumped out of the heart at different pressures depending on the distance to be covered.
• Elasticity of the arteries that allows constrictions which maintains high pressure in arteries.
• Skeletal contractions maintain movement of blood in the veins.
• Thicker walls of arteries which maintain and withstand high pressure of blood.
• Presence of valves in veins and the heart that prevent the back flow of blood.
• Continuous blood supply to the heart muscles by coronary arteries which enables respiration for energy supply to allow the heart contractions.
b) • Transpiration pull
• Root pressure
• Capillary force
• Osmotic pressure
• Cohesion force
• Adhesion force.
answerKey.15:
a) i. Hormones are organic substances secreted in minute quantities into the blood stream by endocrine glands or specialized nerve cells and regulate the growth or functioning of specific tissue.
ii. Endocrine glands
b) Insulin, glucagon
c) When the blood glucose level increases, the beta cells of the pancreas releases insulin hormone. Insulin facilitates conversion of glucose to glycogen and fats, oxidation to glucose. The blood glucose level decreases to the set point. When the blood glucose level decreases, the alpha cells of the pancreas releases glucagon hormone. Glucagon stimulates the conversion of glycogen to glucose, fats to glucose, amino acids to glucose. The blood glucose level increases to the set point.
answerKey.16:
a) Sexual contact with an infected person, use of hypodermic needles contaminated with infected blood, transfusion of infected blood, homosexual partners with infected people, drug users share needles and syringes, infected mothers can infect the foetus.
b) Use of condoms during sexual intercourse, setting up free needle schemes for those people who inject drugs, screening blood before transfusion, education programs to make people aware of the methods of transmission of the HIV virus and how they can be prevented, abstaining from sexual intercourse, faithfulness, avoid drug use, avoid blood contact during accidents, proper delivery of babies during birth (PMTCT).
answerKey.17: Binary fusion, spore formation, vegetative propagation, natural layering, rhizomes, tubers, cutting, fragmentation, budding, grafting, suckers, cloning etc.
answerKey.18:
a) Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the stamen to the stigma of a flower.
b) Self-pollination, cross pollination.
c) Characteristics of self-pollination: • Hermaphrodite flowers
• Anthers and carpels get mature simultaneously,
• Anthers are above the stigma
• Petals remain closed until pollination takes place
• Self-compatibility.
Characteristics of cross pollination: • Anthers mature before stigma
• Self-sterility
• Stigma matures before anthers
answerKey.19:
a) i. Osmosis
ii. It is the movement of water molecules from their highest concentration to the lowest concentration to the lowest concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.
b) i. The water level will rise in the capillary tube.
ii. The solution inside the dialysis tubing exerts osmotic pressure on the water in the beaker and water molecules move from the beaker where there are more water molecules through the dialysis tubing which is acting as a semi-permeable membrane. This will continue until the salts solution is diluted enough. The water level in the beaker will also fall.
c) Water enters both cells because there are more water molecules in the beaker than there are in the cells. The animal cell will eventually swell until it bursts because its cell membrane cannot resist the internal pressure inside the cell. The plant cell however will not burst because it has a cell wall outside the cell membrane which is strong enough to resist the internal pressure inside the cell.

sections.name: A
sections.description: Multiple Choice, True/False, and Fill-in-the-Blank Questions

questions.text: The diagram below represents types of cells.
questions.passage:
[Diagram A: An animal cell showing a nucleus and cell membrane.]
[Diagram B: A plant cell showing a cell wall, cell membrane, and nucleus.]
[Diagram C: A bacterial cell showing a cell membrane.]
[Diagram D: A sperm cell showing a cell membrane and nucleus.]
questions.correctAnswer: A, C and D because they don't have a cell wall, a large central vacuole and chloroplasts.

questions.text: Using √ and X, indicate the parts of the cell found in a plant and animal cell.
questions.passage:
Part | Plant cell | Animal cell
Nucleus | √ | √
Cell membrane | | 
Cytoplasm | | 
Cell wall | | 
Large vacuole | | 
Chloroplasts | |
questions.correctAnswer:
Part | Plant cell | Animal cell
Nucleus | √ | √
Cell membrane | √ | √
Cytoplasm | √ | √
Cell wall | √ | X
Large vacuole | √ | X
Chloroplasts | √ | X

questions.text: Blood contains plasma, red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets. Give the function of each:

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Plasma
subQuestions.correctAnswer: transports nutrients, urea, hormones, blood cells...

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Red blood cells
subQuestions.correctAnswer: transports oxygen and carbon dioxide.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: White blood cells
subQuestions.correctAnswer: body defence, production of antibodies.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: Platelets
subQuestions.correctAnswer: blood clotting.

questions.text: a) Distinguish between saprophytes and parasites.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Distinguish between saprophytes and parasites.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Saprophytes are organisms which feed on dead decaying organic matter while parasites depend on other organisms for both food and shelter. A parasite is an organism which benefits from another (host) causing harm to the host.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: i) Name a common disease in Rwanda caused by protozoa.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium.

subQuestions.label: b) ii)
subQuestions.text: Suggest methods that can be used to control the disease you named in b (i).
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Sleep under mosquito nets; remove stagnant water around homes; spray with insecticides; remove bushes around houses.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The vascular systems of a flowering plant?
subQuestions.correctAnswer:
Xylem: water and mineral salts
Phloem: water, sucrose, amino acids

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The blood system of mammals?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Water, blood proteins, glucose, fats, amino acids, salts, vitamins, carbon dioxide, blood cells, oxygen, hormones, waste products.

questions.text: a) Why is mucus needed to protect the cell lining the gut from protein-digesting enzymes?

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Why is mucus needed to protect the cell lining the gut from protein-digesting enzymes?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Because cells are partly made of proteins and would be broken down into digestion and would cause ulcers.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The pH in the stomach is very acidic about pH 2. Why would it be a problem if the pH in the small intestines was 2?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: This is a wrong PH for the enzymes in the gut because they would be denatured by acidity and would not work or the enzymes of the small intestines work at basic (alkaline) PH.

questions.text: An experiment was done to investigate effects of bile on lipid digestion.
questions.passage: Lipase enzyme and bile were added to a lipid test tube A. Test tube B only had the lipid and lipase enzyme.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Explain why the pH in test tube A became acidic.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Lipase breaks down lipids facilitated by bile salts of bile to fatty acids and glycerol. The emulsification of fats increases the surface area of lipids; this facilitates the activity of lipase enzymes. Fatty acids are acidic and lowers the PH.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Explain why the reaction in test tube B was very slow.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Because there was no bile to emulsify the lipids for easy digestion or because there is no bile to facilitate lipase action.

questions.text: Explain why the elimination of water by the kidney may be considered to be both excretion and osmoregulation.
questions.correctAnswer: Excretion of water is a metabolic product and is removed when in excess and osmoregulation in water is maintained to keep the water potential of blood more or less constant or to maintain water balance, maintain normal blood concentration.

questions.text: Why is it more accurate to describe fish as a ‘variable-temperature’ animal rather than cold-blooded?
questions.correctAnswer: Its body temperature is the same as or only a few degrees above its surroundings. This makes it very dependent on temperature changes. Or the temperature of the fish changes depending on the surroundings.

questions.text: a) How is a zygote different from any other cell in the body?

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: How is a zygote different from any other cell in the body?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A zygote is a product of fusion of male and female reproductive cells. All other cells of the body arises from the mitotic division of the zygote.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Why is it difficult to decide whether viruses are living organisms?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: A virus when outside a cell cannot grow or reproduce. It does not feed, respire or excrete. These are characteristics of living things. It is for this reason that it is difficult to say whether it is living or not. The only living characteristic it shows is the ability to reproduce when inside a living cell, using materials supplied by the cells.

questions.text: The diagram below shows a villus in longitudinal section from ileum of a mammal.
questions.passage: [Diagram of a villus with a scale bar labeled 0.2 mm. Label P points to the capillary network. Label Q points to the lacteal.]

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Name the parts labelled P and Q.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: P - Epithelium (blood capillary network); Q - Lacteal (lymphatic vessel)

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: What are the functions of P and Q?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: P absorbs amino acids and glucose and glucose/vitamins/mineral salts. Or transport, glucose/amino acids /mineral salts. Q absorbs fatty acids and glycerol.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Explain how P is adapted to carry out its functions.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The lining of the epithelium is very thin or permeable membrane, extensive capillary network, rich blood supply etc.

questions.text: The diagram below shows the side view of a skull of a carnivore.
questions.passage: [Diagram of a carnivore skull in profile. Label A points to the canine tooth. Label B points to the carnassial tooth. Label C points to the incisor tooth.]
questions.correctAnswer: A - incisors, B - Canine, C - Carnassials or molars

questions.text: Choose words from the list to complete the sentences that follow.
questions.passage: Cells; Chloroplast; Chromosomes; Genes; Organs; Organisms; Nucleus; Tissues.
questions.correctAnswer: i) nucleus, ii) chromosomes, iii) genes, iv) tissues, v) organs, vi) organisms

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The flow of blood is maintained in a mammal.
subQuestions.correctAnswer:
Maintenance of blood flow in a mammal
• The automatic stimulation of the pace maker that enables the continuous contraction of the cardiac muscles that allows the pumping of blood out of the heart to the rest of the body parts.
• Difference in ventricular muscles which enables blood to be pumped out of the heart at different pressures depending on the distance to be covered.
• Elasticity of the arteries that allows constrictions which maintains high pressure in arteries.
• Skeletal contractions maintain movement of blood in the veins.
• Thicker walls of arteries which maintain and withstand high pressure of blood.
• Presence of valves in veins and the heart that prevent the back flow of blood.
• Continuous blood supply to the heart muscles by coronary arteries which enables respiration for energy supply to allow the heart contractions.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The flow of water is maintained in a flowering plant.
subQuestions.correctAnswer:
• Transpiration pull
• Root pressure
• Capillary force
• Osmotic pressure
• Cohesion force
• Adhesion force.

subQuestions.label: a) i)
subQuestions.text: What are hormones?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Hormones are organic substances secreted in minute quantities into the blood stream by endocrine glands or specialized nerve cells and regulate the growth or functioning of specific tissue.

subQuestions.label: a) ii)
subQuestions.text: Where are hormones produced?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Endocrine glands

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Which hormones are produced by the pancreas?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Insulin, glucagon

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Explain how the blood sugar level in blood is controlled.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: When the blood glucose level increases, the beta cells of the pancreas releases insulin hormone. Insulin facilitates conversion of glucose to glycogen and fats, oxidation to glucose. The blood glucose level decreases to the set point. When the blood glucose level decreases, the alpha cells of the pancreas releases glucagon hormone. Glucagon stimulates the conversion of glycogen to glucose, fats to glucose, amino acids to glucose. The blood glucose level increases to the set point.

questions.text: a) How is AIDS transmitted from one person to another?

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: How is AIDS transmitted from one person to another?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Sexual contact with an infected person, use of hypodermic needles contaminated with infected blood, transfusion of infected blood, homosexual partners with infected people, drug users share needles and syringes, infected mothers can infect the foetus.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Suggest all possible methods that can be used to avoid the AIDS virus.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Use of condoms during sexual intercourse, setting up free needle schemes for those people who inject drugs, screening blood before transfusion, education programs to make people aware of the methods of transmission of the HIV virus and how they can be prevented, abstaining from sexual intercourse, faithfulness, avoid drug use, avoid blood contact during accidents, proper delivery of babies during birth (PMTCT).

questions.text: Identify the various modes of asexual reproduction in plants and animals.
questions.correctAnswer: Binary fusion, spore formation, vegetative propagation, natural layering, rhizomes, tubers, cutting, fragmentation, budding, grafting, suckers, cloning etc.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define pollination.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the stamen to the stigma of a flower.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Name the types of pollination and characteristics of each.
subQuestions.correctAnswer:
Self-pollination, cross pollination.
Characteristics of self-pollination: • Hermaphrodite flowers
• Anthers and carpels get mature simultaneously,
• Anthers are above the stigma
• Petals remain closed until pollination takes place
• Self-compatibility.
Characteristics of cross pollination: • Anthers mature before stigma
• Self-sterility
• Stigma matures before anthers

questions.text: Some students set the experiment below to investigate a biological process.
questions.passage: [Diagram: A beaker containing water. Inside the beaker is a thistle funnel with a capillary tube inserted into its stem. The bottom of the funnel is covered by a membrane labeled 'Dialysis tubing containing concentrated salt solution'. An arrow points to the liquid level in the capillary tube labeled 'Level of solution at the start of the investigation'. Labels include 'Capillary tube', 'Beaker', and 'Water'.]

subQuestions.label: a) i)
subQuestions.text: Name the process being investigated.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Osmosis

subQuestions.label: a) ii)
subQuestions.text: Define the process named in a(i) above.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: It is the movement of water molecules from their highest concentration to the lowest concentration to the lowest concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.

subQuestions.label: b) i)
subQuestions.text: What will happen to the water level in the capillary tube during this investigation?
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The water level will rise in the capillary tube.

subQuestions.label: b) ii)
subQuestions.text: Explain what has happened in b(i) above.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: The solution inside the dialysis tubing exerts osmotic pressure on the water in the beaker and water molecules move from the beaker where there are more water molecules through the dialysis tubing which is acting as a semi-permeable membrane. This will continue until the salts solution is diluted enough. The water level in the beaker will also fall.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Explain fully what happens when both animal and plant cells are put in beaker containing pure water.
subQuestions.correctAnswer: Water enters both cells because there are more water molecules in the beaker than there are in the cells. The animal cell will eventually swell until it bursts because its cell membrane cannot resist the internal pressure inside the cell. The plant cell however will not burst because it has a cell wall outside the cell membrane which is strong enough to resist the internal pressure inside the cell.
